[METADATA/INTRO]: World War II photography marked a turning point in visual documentation of conflict. Advancements in camera technology and the rise of picture magazines revolutionized how war was captured and presented to the public. Military photography units, iconic photographers like Robert...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: - Captured iconic D-Day landing images on Omaha Beach 
 - Co-founded Magnum Photos agency after the war 
 - Developed the concept of the "concerned photographer" engaged with world events 
 - First female war correspondent accredited by the U.S. Army 
 - Photographed the liberation of Buchenwald concentration camp 
 - Captured industrial and social subjects on the home front and abroad 
 - Known for her dramatic compositions and use of large format cameras 
 - Associated Press photographer who took the famous Iwo Jima flag-raising photo 
 - Won the Pulitzer Prize for Photography in 1945 
 - Controversy surrounded the staging of the second flag-raising

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: Top Secret: How Allied Airmen Evaded Capture
[METADATA/INTRO]: by Adam Lynch A few moments after his stricken Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress bomber tore apart, co-pilot Ralph Patton hurriedly put his bail-out plan into action. ; [text_ad]...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: About midnight, the men were led from the house to a spot about two kilometers away, close to the top of a 200-foot cliff above the beach. This part of the French coast was studded with German gun emplacements, so the group was warned to stay on the narrow path and keep quiet as they moved along. Before starting down, they were told to dig their heels in to avoid falling in the darkness. Halfway down, Dumais directed the quick flashlight to signal the letter “B” out over the water to the waiting boat. 
 Was it really going to happen? Was this little band of evaders finally going to get out of occupied Europe and back to England? Well, not right away. After successfully reaching what the French called Bonaparte Beach, the group of evaders, agents, and Shelbourne helpers, now numbering 35 people altogether, sat on large rocks at the foot of the cliff in the cold and waited … and waited. With each passing wave the little band of flyers imagined they could see an approaching boat. Suddenly, the sky turned bright as day, followed a second later by the thundering roar of big guns. The German coastal batteries were firing into the night. Patton says, “ It scared the daylights out us.” The group feared the guns were shooting at the British gunboat now holding about a mile off shore, but apparently it was only practice firing.
